ideaItems.title: Caryina — customers can request returns but staff have no way to manage or respond to them
ideaItems.body: When customers request a return, their request goes into a void. Staff have no visibility into pending returns, no way to approve or reject them, and no process to issue a return label or trigger a refund. Every return requires manual intervention outside the system.

ideaItems.title: Brikette — Nobody is tracking whether the staging site is getting too large
ideaItems.body: As the site grows, the amount of code sent to visitors' browsers can creep up without anyone noticing. Too much code means slower page loads and a worse booking experience for guests. Setting up automatic size tracking means we catch this early, before it starts affecting anyone.

ideaItems.title: Reception — A guest with no payment history shows as a free extension with no warning
ideaItems.body: If payment data hasn't been loaded for a booking, staff see a €0.00 price and might assume the extension is complimentary. They could process the extension and collect nothing when the guest should have been charged.

ideaItems.title: Reception — Extension booking can be submitted with 0 extra nights, silently doing nothing
ideaItems.body: Staff can accidentally type 0 in the nights field and click Extend. The form accepts it without complaint, processes nothing, and gives no feedback. Fixing this ensures the form always requires at least 1 night before the Extend button is available.

ideaItems.title: The Gmail auth expiry fix is reactive. A proactive health-check process (periodic credential validity probe, alerting before expiry rather than after failure) would be a natural follow-on. Not actioned by this build — candidate for a future fact-find.
ideaItems.body: Build review surfaced "The Gmail auth expiry fix is reactive. A proactive health-check process (periodic credential validity probe, alerting before expiry rather than after failure) would be a natural follow-on. Not actioned by this build — candidate for a future fact-find." with supporting narrative: New loop process — The Gmail auth expiry fix is reactive. A proactive health-check process (periodic credential validity probe, alerting before expiry rather than after failure) would be a natural follow-on. Not actioned by this build — candidate for a future fact-find.

build_origin.canonical_title: The Gmail auth expiry fix is reactive. A proactive health-check process (periodic credential validity probe, alerting before expiry rather than after failure) would be a natural follow-on. Not actioned by this build — candidate for a future fact-find.

ideaItems.title: Prime guest app — the owner dashboard gives no warning when the data pipeline has stopped collecting figures
ideaItems.body: The owner dashboard shows occupancy and revenue figures. When there is no data, it shows a blank message. But the same blank message appears whether the period genuinely had no guests or whether the data collection process quietly stopped working. An owner could go weeks without noticing that their KPIs are broken.

ideaItems.title: Add a pre-push broken-symlink hygiene check to the repo's validate-changes gates. Trigger observation: the planned wave commit was blocked by a pre-existing broken symlink under .agents/skills/, requiring an out-of-scope fix commit before CI could proceed. Suggested next action: create card.
ideaItems.body: Build review surfaced "Add a pre-push broken-symlink hygiene check to the repo's validate-changes gates. Trigger observation: the planned wave commit was blocked by a pre-existing broken symlink under .agents/skills/, requiring an out-of-scope fix commit before CI could proceed. Suggested next action: create card." with supporting narrative: New loop process — Add a pre-push broken-symlink hygiene check to the repo's validate-changes gates. Trigger observation: the planned wave commit was blocked by a pre-existing broken symlink under .agents/skills/, requiring an out-of-scope fix commit before CI could proceed. Suggested next action: create card.

Trigger observation: the planned wave commit was blocked by a pre-existing broken symlink under .agents/skills/, requiring an out-of-scope fix commit before CI could proceed. Suggested next action: create card.

ideaItems.title: Platform — Ideas queue can't tell when two different dispatches are about the same actual change
ideaItems.body: When the system goes live, the same real change could trigger multiple dispatches from different angles. Without content-aware dedup, duplicate investigations get queued, wasting time and creating conflicting plans. SHA-based matching would catch these and consolidate them into one piece of work.

ideaItems.title: XA — When the upload tool breaks in production, there is nothing to investigate
ideaItems.body: The product upload pipeline currently only sends messages to the developer console, which is invisible in a live environment. When something goes wrong for real users, there is no record of what happened or why. Adding proper logging means any failure leaves a clear trail, making problems far faster to diagnose and fix.

ideaItems.title: Business OS — A manual code migration pattern is a candidate for full automation
ideaItems.body: A particular style of code update has been repeated manually multiple times using an identical process. While mostly finished for now, automating it would eliminate the manual effort entirely if the pattern comes back. The case is early but the opportunity is clear — keeping it tracked prevents it being forgotten.

build_origin.canonical_title: Possible: a "mutation hook migration" skill could automate the pattern of reading hook files, detecting pattern type (A/B/C/D/E), and applying the correct migration template. Low priority given Pattern D hooks are deferred and this migration is mostly complete.

ideaItems.title: Brikette — Recovering stuck guest emails requires a staff member to manually trigger the fix
ideaItems.body: When the AI gets stuck processing a guest email, a staff member has to manually run a recovery step. If they are busy or forget, the guest waits indefinitely with no reply. Running this recovery step automatically on a schedule means no guest message stays stuck, without anyone needing to remember to action it.

ideaItems.title: Brikette reception — check-in booking emails land in Gmail as drafts, never reach guests
ideaItems.body: Guests need their check-in app-link to access digital services. The link is generated and put into a Gmail draft, but someone has to manually find and send it from Gmail. If they forget, the guest never gets the link. Making the system send it immediately means every guest receives their link as soon as the button is pressed, with no manual follow-up needed.

ideaItems.title: Brikette reception — Gmail login failures show staff a vague error instead of actionable guidance
ideaItems.body: All guest email sending relies on a single Gmail login credential. If that credential expires, emails silently fail. Staff see a vague error and don’t know what to do. Making the error message specific means staff can immediately escalate to the right person and no guest emails are unknowingly delayed while someone guesses at the cause.

ideaItems.title: Brikette reception — no record of who sent which guest reminder or when
ideaItems.body: If a guest says they were never contacted, there is currently no way to check whether a staff member actually marked the reminder as sent, or to see who pressed the button and when. Adding the staff member’s identity and a timestamp to the activity record means every action is traceable. Managers can answer guest disputes and spot patterns if the same step is being skipped.

ideaItems.title: Brikette reception — short unsellable gaps between bookings look the same as open availability
ideaItems.body: When there is a single free night sandwiched between two bookings, it almost certainly cannot be filled because most guests stay at least two nights. But on the room grid, that tiny gap looks exactly the same as a whole empty week. Highlighting short gaps in a different colour lets staff immediately see where availability is fragmented and decide whether to try adjusting adjacent bookings, change the minimum stay, or simply accept the lost night.

ideaItems.title: Caryina — no customer reviews or social proof to help shoppers trust a new brand
ideaItems.body: Shoppers on a brand they've never heard of rely heavily on reviews and social signals to decide whether to trust it with their money. When there are none, a significant share of visitors who were nearly ready to buy will hesitate. Even a handful of real testimonials — or a link to a social account with real posts — meaningfully improves conversion for a new brand.

ideaItems.title: Caryina — brand credibility damaged by a hostel email address as the customer contact
ideaItems.body: When a fashion brand's only contact email is a hostel address, it looks like a dropshipping side project rather than a real brand. A customer with a problem who sees 'hostelpositano@gmail.com' may not trust the business enough to contact it — or may report the site as suspicious. A brand email address (like hello@caryina.com) costs almost nothing and has an outsized effect on perceived legitimacy.

ideaItems.title: Brikette guest app — guests have no way to stop other guests from messaging them
ideaItems.body: Right now any guest in the hostel can send a direct message to any other guest. There is no way for a guest to say they want to be left alone. Some guests travel for work or simply prefer privacy — this feature lets them opt out of peer messages without any staff involvement.

ideaItems.title: Brikette — broadcast channel ID is written twice in two places and they can drift apart silently
ideaItems.body: If someone renames the broadcast channel ID in one file, the other file still uses the old name and will silently route broadcasts incorrectly. This is a one-line import change that removes the duplication.

ideaItems.title: Brikette guest app — a single flag can disable the staff login check entirely, even in production
ideaItems.body: A configuration flag that was meant only for development testing can accidentally (or intentionally) disable the staff login check in the live guest app. This is a single-line logic error that, if triggered, removes all access control from the staff portal.

ideaItems.title: Brikette guest app — broadcast messages can be sent without any identity or safety checks
ideaItems.body: Staff can send a broadcast to all guests without the app checking who they are or stopping repeated sends. This means anyone with basic access to the staff tools could flood guest messages, and there would be no record of who sent what or when. The fix brings this endpoint up to the same standard as the other staff-only actions.

ideaItems.title: Brikette guest app — most staff actions can hang indefinitely if the guest system becomes unresponsive
ideaItems.body: If the guest app is slow, staff trying to save a draft or send a message to a specific guest can get stuck waiting indefinitely with no feedback. Adding timeouts means a clear error appears after 10 seconds so staff can retry instead of wondering if it worked.

ideaItems.title: Brikette guest app — staff identity headers can silently disappear, causing all actions to fail with no explanation
ideaItems.body: If a staff member's login session is missing their ID (an edge case that can happen), all attempts to take action in the guest app silently fail with a confusing error. Staff have no idea why it stopped working. Fixing this means the problem surfaces immediately with a clear message instead of failing silently.

ideaItems.title: Brikette guest app — staff roles can be silently rejected for minor formatting differences, blocking access with no explanation
ideaItems.body: If a staff member's role appears in the system with a small formatting difference — a space, different capitalisation — they get blocked from the guest tools with no explanation. Support has no easy way to diagnose it. Normalising roles before checking them means these trivial differences never cause a lockout.

ideaItems.title: Reception — Extending an entire shared booking can leave some guests extended and others not if anything goes wrong mid-save
ideaItems.body: If a multi-guest extension is interrupted halfway through — by a network blip or any other error — some guests end up extended and others don't. The booking data is now inconsistent and staff have no easy way to know who was extended.

ideaItems.title: Reception — Extension price is too low when some guests in a booking have already left
ideaItems.body: When a group booking has some guests leave early and others stay on, the extension price is split across everyone who was ever in the booking — including those who left. Staff collect less than the correct amount for the remaining guests.

ideaItems.title: Reception — Rooms not recognised by the system always appear available for extension
ideaItems.body: If a guest is assigned to a room that isn't set up in the system, the extension page shows them as available to extend — even if the room is full or fictional. Staff could extend a guest into a room that can't actually hold them.

ideaItems.title: Reception — The button to extend an entire booking uses a nights value that isn't clearly linked to it
ideaItems.body: Staff might set different night counts in different rows expecting each person to get a different extension, then click the group Booking button and have everyone extended by the same number. The page gives no clue this is happening.

ideaItems.title: Reception — The city tax rate is buried in the code with no easy way to update it
ideaItems.body: Italian city tax rates are set by the municipality and change periodically. Having the number buried in the code means it can be missed in an update. A named constant makes it easy to find and change when the rate changes.

ideaItems.title: Reception — The extension page gets slower every month as the booking history grows
ideaItems.body: As the hostel builds up booking history over months and years, the extension page will take increasingly longer to become usable. Limiting the scan to a sensible window keeps the page fast regardless of how much history has accumulated.

ideaItems.title: Reception — The nights-to-extend number fields have no label for screen readers
ideaItems.body: Staff who use assistive technology see unlabelled number boxes on the extension page with no way to know what they're for. Adding a label fixes this immediately.

ideaItems.title: Reception — When the extension page fails to load, staff can't tell what went wrong
ideaItems.body: When the extension page fails, staff see one unhelpful message and have no idea what to do. If the issue is with just one data source, partial information might still be usable. Specific error messages help staff decide whether to wait, retry, or call for support.

ideaItems.title: BRIK — Cleaning staff can't signal a room is ready, leaving reception guessing at check-in
ideaItems.body: Right now, cleaning staff have no button or action to say a room is finished being cleaned. Front desk staff checking guests in can't see on their screen whether a guest's specific room is actually ready. This means reception either has to call out to cleaning staff or just hope the room is ready when a guest arrives — both cause delays and awkward moments for guests. Adding a simple 'mark as done' step for cleaners, and showing that status per-room on the check-in screen, means reception always knows exactly which rooms are ready before a guest reaches the desk.

ideaItems.title: Caryina — four payment setup steps still needed before real transactions can work
ideaItems.body: A payment audit identified four issues that are intentionally deferred until the Stripe and Axerve accounts are properly configured: (1) Stripe and Axerve credentials need real keys — the current defaults will reject every payment attempt; (2) the Stripe webhook secret is a placeholder — all post-payment confirmations that arrive via webhook are silently rejected; (3) Axerve has no 3DS security challenge — EU banks will decline most card payments under PSD2 rules; (4) order confirmation emails are silently not sent — the merchant has no record of purchases. These are noted here so they are picked up as part of the payment provider go-live work.

ideaItems.title: Platform — Agents produce generic-looking UI because skills restrict every creative lever to token defaults
ideaItems.body: Agent skills actively discourage the creative choices that would make each brand distinctive. Requesting a specific style produces almost no visible change.

ideaItems.title: Platform — Apps cannot express their brand identity because the design system has no way to store art-direction choices
ideaItems.body: Every app looks the same because the theming system can only store colour values, not the broader creative choices that make a brand recognisable. Agents default to the same rounded cards and blue buttons regardless of the brand.

ideaItems.title: deploy-order gate for sequential service dependencies
ideaItems.body: Build review surfaced "deploy-order gate for sequential service dependencies" with supporting narrative: TASK-07 carries a hard deploy-order requirement (Reception TASK-02 must be confirmed in production before TASK-07 is merged); this enforcement currently relies entirely on the PR description note with no automated check

build_origin.canonical_title: deploy-order gate for sequential service dependencies

ideaItems.title: signed-claims header verification is already fully deterministic
ideaItems.body: Build review surfaced "signed-claims header verification is already fully deterministic" with supporting narrative: verifyActorClaims and signActorClaims are pure WebCrypto functions; no LLM step involved in verification flow — this is an existing mechanistic pattern, not a candidate for replacement

build_origin.canonical_title: signed-claims header verification is already fully deterministic

ideaItems.title: Gmail credential health-check — proactive alert before auth expiry
ideaItems.body: Build review surfaced recurring follow-up work: Gmail credential health-check — proactive alert before auth expiry

build_origin.canonical_title: Gmail credential health-check — proactive alert before auth expiry

ideaItems.title: Possible: Container/Inline codemod. The fix pattern for container-widths-only-at (replace with ) and for enforce-layout-primitives (replace on leaf elements with ) are deterministic enough to be implemented as a jscodeshift codemod. Would eliminate LLM reasoning for the mechanical substitution step.

ideaItems.title: Possible: DS-Lint Batch Fix Skill. This build repeated a clear pattern: enumerate violations with dry-run lint, apply per-rule fix patterns (Container swap, Inline/Stack for leaf flex/grid, min-h-10 min-w-10 for tap targets) across clusters, validate with full lint. A parameterized skill for "fix all DS lint suppression debts in a given app" could automate this reliably and reduce build cycle time from hours to minutes for future DS compliance recovery sprints.
ideaItems.body: Build review surfaced "Possible: DS-Lint Batch Fix Skill. A parameterized skill for "fix all DS lint suppression debts in a given app" could automate this reliably and reduce build cycle time from hours to minutes for future DS compliance recovery sprints." with supporting narrative: New skill — Possible: DS-Lint Batch Fix Skill. This build repeated a clear pattern: enumerate violations with dry-run lint, apply per-rule fix patterns (Container swap, Inline/Stack for leaf flex/grid, min-h-10 min-w-10 for tap targets) across clusters, validate with full lint. A parameterized skill for "fix all DS lint suppression debts in a given app" could automate this reliably and reduce build cycle time from hours to minutes for future DS compliance recovery sprints.

This build repeated a clear pattern: enumerate violations with dry-run lint, apply per-rule fix patterns (Container swap, Inline/Stack for leaf flex/grid, min-h-10 min-w-10 for tap targets) across clusters, validate with full lint. A parameterized skill for "fix all DS lint suppression debts in a given app" could automate this reliably and reduce build cycle time from hours to minutes for future DS compliance recovery sprints.

ideaItems.title: Candidate: /lp-security-remediation — recurring workflow: when GATE-LAUNCH-SEC blocks due to status: fail on specific SEC checks (e.g., SEC-02 missing headers, SEC-04 bad cookie flags), there is a repeatable remediation + re-audit loop. Once a second business hits S9B this pattern will repeat. Trigger observation: s9b-gates.md step 4 defines a hard block path with "Remediate all failing security checks (SEC-XX)"; no guided remediation skill exists yet.

ideaItems.title: Candidate: A ds-compliance-sweep skill that runs grep + ESLint + tsc to produce a targeted list of remaining DS violations (raw buttons, inline styles, layout warnings) could be useful before future theming passes. The violation-enumeration work (fact-find phase) was substantial for this plan; a deterministic script could produce the same list in seconds. Trigger: this build's fact-find enumerated 35 raw buttons, 14 ESLint warnings, and 6 inline styles via manual grep — fully mechanizable.

ideaItems.title: The domain classification in the cleanup script (inferDomain()) uses deterministic keyword heuristics to classify domains from area_anchor text. This replaces what was previously an implicit LLM judgment during dispatch creation. Trigger observation: 291 dispatches classified with 0 remaining gaps using a ~80-line function.
ideaItems.body: Build review surfaced "The domain classification in the cleanup script (inferDomain()) uses deterministic keyword heuristics to classify domains from area_anchor text. This replaces what was previously an implicit LLM judgment during dispatch creation. Trigger observation: 291 dispatches classified with 0 remaining gaps using a ~80-line function." with supporting narrative: AI-to-mechanistic — The domain classification in the cleanup script (inferDomain()) uses deterministic keyword heuristics to classify domains from area_anchor text. This replaces what was previously an implicit LLM judgment during dispatch creation. Trigger observation: 291 dispatches classified with 0 remaining gaps using a ~80-line function.

build_origin.canonical_title: The domain classification in the cleanup script (inferDomain()) uses deterministic keyword heuristics to classify domains from area_anchor text. This replaces what was previously an implicit LLM judgment during dispatch creation. Trigger observation: 291 dispatches classified with 0 remaining gaps using a ~80-line function.

ideaItems.title: Brikette — Check-in view badges and loan button use undefined colour tokens
ideaItems.body: Check-in is where staff manage arriving guests. The status badges that tell staff whether a guest is confirmed, pending, or has a problem use colour names that don't exist in the theme — so they may render incorrectly or not at all. The loan button has a similar issue. Fixing this ensures check-in status indicators are always visible and correct.

ideaItems.title: Brikette — Prepayment and management screens use shorthand colours and opacity hacks
ideaItems.body: These screens are used daily for managing bookings, payments, stock, and staff. Warning badges and error messages use informal colour shorthand that may not resolve correctly in the theme, creating visual inconsistency. Some opacity values create nearly-invisible backgrounds. Fixing this means all management screens feel polished and consistent with the rest of the app.

ideaItems.title: Brikette — Sidebar navigation uses see-through backgrounds that look unfinished
ideaItems.body: The sidebar navigation appears on every screen. Its see-through background can make text hard to read depending on what's behind it. Defining proper overlay tokens means the sidebar always looks clean and readable regardless of the page content underneath.

ideaItems.title: Brikette — Changing language on the guides page causes a noticeable slowdown on slower phones
ideaItems.body: When a guest switches language on the guides listing page, the search system rebuilds its entire index from scratch. On slower phones this creates a brief but noticeable lag. Investigating and fixing the cause would ensure the guides page feels fast for all guests, regardless of the device they are using.

ideaItems.title: Brikette — Four key pages share copy-pasted code that already has a bug in all of them
ideaItems.body: Four pages on the site use identical code blocks that were manually copied between them. A bug introduced once quietly spread to all four pages without anyone noticing. If a fifth page is added, the same broken code will spread again. Extracting this into a single shared function fixes the bug once and prevents it from spreading further.

ideaItems.title: Business OS — Features are described as available before the infrastructure to support them exists
ideaItems.body: New capabilities are being written into our planning documents before the actual code and systems they depend on have been built. This means automation tools can be pointed at features that do not fully exist yet, leading to wasted work and confusion. Keeping capability descriptions tied to what is proven and working prevents this gap from growing.

build_origin.canonical_title: Capability references drift ahead of the artifact and schema they require

ideaItems.title: Brikette — A colour setting is written out in multiple places and will drift out of sync
ideaItems.body: The same list of colours used in the PIN entry screen is written separately in several parts of the codebase. If we ever need to change it, every copy has to be found and updated individually — and it is easy to miss one. Putting it in one shared place means any change takes seconds and stays consistent everywhere.

ideaItems.title: Brikette — Staging site deployments take longer than they need to
ideaItems.body: Every time we test a change on the staging site, the entire build starts from scratch — even the parts that have not changed. Caching the unchanged parts means faster feedback on what is working, so less time is spent waiting between making a change and seeing it on the staging site.

ideaItems.title: Brikette — There is no running record of how the email system recovers from problems
ideaItems.body: When the guest email system hits an error and recovers, there is no log showing what happened or how it was resolved. Without this, we cannot spot patterns or know if the same problem keeps recurring. A simple running record of these events makes it much easier to improve the system over time.

ideaItems.title: Business OS — A colour accessibility check is done by hand when it could run automatically
ideaItems.body: Every time a colour is changed in the design system, someone has to manually run an accessibility check to make sure text stays readable against the new colour. This step has already been missed once. Making it run automatically before any colour change is saved means the check can never be skipped, however busy things are.

build_origin.canonical_title: The contrast pre-verification step (running pnpm tokens:contrast:check with proposed values before committing) could be a mechanistic pre-build check rather than a manual task step. Trigger observation: TASK-01 Red step required manual verification; this could be automated as a pre-token-edit gate. Signal is weak (single occurrence); not actionable yet.

ideaItems.title: Business OS — New quality rules can be declared working before they are actually tested
ideaItems.body: When we add a new quality rule to our automation tools, it can be marked as active and ready before any automated check confirms it works. Rules that sound good on paper can slip through unproven, creating a false sense of safety. Requiring a working test before any rule goes live means we only ship improvements that are genuinely verified.

build_origin.canonical_title: require executable audit coverage before new heuristics graduate from skill prose

ideaItems.title: Brikette — PIN screen colour settings are duplicated and will eventually get out of sync
ideaItems.body: The list of colours used for the PIN entry display appears in multiple separate places in the codebase. When it needs updating, each copy has to be found and changed individually, and it is easy to miss one. Bringing this into a single shared location means any update applies everywhere at once.

ideaItems.title: Brikette — Post-deployment health checks only happen if someone remembers to run them
ideaItems.body: After we release an update, certain background systems need to be checked to confirm they are running correctly. This check currently only happens if someone actively remembers to do it. Making it automatic after every deployment means problems are caught quickly, before staff or guests notice anything wrong.

ideaItems.title: Brikette — Recovery events from the email system leave no trace
ideaItems.body: When the guest email system encounters a problem and fixes itself, that event disappears with no record. Without a log, we cannot tell how often this happens or whether things are getting better or worse over time. Capturing these events creates a learning trail that makes the system progressively more reliable.

ideaItems.title: XA — The product publishing confirmation screen looks unfinished
ideaItems.body: When staff prepare to publish products to the store, the final confirmation screen uses a plain unstyled list with no grouping. It doesn't match the polished look of the rest of the tool, making it harder to quickly check what's about to go live. Bringing it in line with the rest of the app makes the step clearer and reduces the chance of publishing the wrong items.

ideaItems.title: Brikette — Old email folders pile up and clutter the staff inbox view
ideaItems.body: Every time we rename or reorganise how guest emails are labelled, the old folder names stay in the email system forever. Over time staff have to scroll past a growing list of abandoned folders to find what they need. Cleaning these up automatically keeps the email workspace tidy and easy to navigate.

ideaItems.title: XA — Empty basket and no-results screens break the premium brand feel
ideaItems.body: When a shopper has an empty basket or a search returns nothing, they see plain text with no visual care. These moments are small but telling — they are the brand speaking during a moment of friction. Giving them a designed treatment keeps the premium feel consistent all the way through the shopping journey.

ideaItems.title: PWRB — No physical hardware unit has been chosen for the product yet
ideaItems.body: The PWRB service will use physical hardware that customers interact with, but no specific unit has been selected or sourced yet. Without a chosen product that meets European safety standards, there is nothing to test, price, or show customers. Selecting and ordering the hardware unblocks all downstream product and pricing work.

ideaItems.title: PWRB — No pilot locations have been identified to test with real customers
ideaItems.body: The PWRB service needs real venues to run its first pilots, but none have been shortlisted or approached yet. Without at least one location committed, there is nowhere to test the product, demonstrate it, or validate that customers want it. Choosing a venue shortlist unblocks the entire go-to-market plan.

ideaItems.title: PWRB — We don't know how long payment holds will stay valid before charging
ideaItems.body: For the PWRB service to work, customers' payments need to be put on hold before the session begins and charged afterwards. Payment providers only allow holds for a limited time window, and if we exceed it the charge will fail. Confirming exactly how long we have prevents a billing failure the very first time a customer pays.
